tasks.description: Create `src/tools/video-editor/lib/overhang.ts` with `detectClipOverhang` and `getMediaEndFraction` utilities. Import `getClipSourceDuration` from `config-utils.ts` (line 17) for total source duration. ClipMeta type is in `timeline-data.ts:27`. For clips with `hold` property or `clipType === 'hold'`, return no overhang. For video clips: `overhangSource = Math.max(0, (to ?? 0) - assetDuration)`, `overhangTimeline = overhangSource / (speed ?? 1)`. If assetDuration is undefined/null, return no overhang. `getMediaEndFraction` returns `1 - (overhangTimelineSeconds / totalTimelineDuration)` where totalTimelineDuration = `getClipSourceDuration(clipMeta) / (speed ?? 1)`. Returns null if no overhang or duration unknown. Create `overhang.test.ts` with cases: no overhang, basic overhang (from=0 speed=1), overhang with nonzero from, speed=2, speed=0.5, hold clips, missing assetDuration, getMediaEndFraction fraction correctness.

Add `clampClipToMediaDuration` and `convertOverhangToHold` helpers to `overhang.ts`. `clampClipToMediaDuration(clipMeta, assetDuration)` returns a `Partial<ClipMeta>` patch setting `to = Math.min(to, assetDuration)`. If no overhang exists, return empty patch. `convertOverhangToHold(clipMeta, assetDuration)` returns `{ videoPatch, holdClip }` where videoPatch clamps `to = assetDuration` and holdClip = `{ from: 0, to: 0, hold: overhangTimelineSeconds, clipType: 'hold' }` — the hold clip occupies the tail duration that was dead time. The hold clip inherits the same asset for thumbnail purposes. Add tests: no overhang returns empty patch, speed != 1, from != 0, holdClip duration correctness.

Improve duration resolution on shot drop in `useExternalDrop.ts`. At line 117-126 where `resolveFinalVideoDurationSeconds()` is called: if it returns null, add a single retry after ~500ms using `extractVideoMetadataFromUrl` (already imported in `finalVideoAssets.ts:129` from `src/shared/lib/media/videoMetadata.ts`). If still null, keep the existing 5s fallback. If duration resolves, ensure `to` does not exceed the resolved duration. Key: `extractVideoMetadataFromUrl` is the same function used inside `resolveFinalVideoDurationSeconds` at `finalVideoAssets.ts:129` — import it directly for the retry.

Clamp on switch-to-final-video in `shot-group-commands.ts`. At lines 331-335 in `buildSwitchShotGroupToFinalVideoMutation`: the existing `Math.min(imageSpanDuration, resolvedVideoDuration ?? imageSpanDuration)` is already correct when duration is known. Verify `buildUpdateShotGroupVideoMutation` (~line 430) applies the same clamping. Wire duration into `buildFinalVideoAssetEntry` in `finalVideoAssets.ts:136-149` so the asset registry entry includes `duration` when available — this is critical for the overhang indicator in Phase 3 to detect issues. Check the `AssetRegistryEntry` type to confirm it has a `duration` field.

Pass overhang data to clip renderer. In `TimelineEditor.tsx` at the `getActionRender` callback (line 711): look up `assetDuration` from the data ref using the clip's asset key against the registry. Compute `mediaEndFraction` using `getMediaEndFraction(clipMeta, assetDuration)` from the new overhang utility. Pass `mediaEndFraction` and a `hasOverhang` boolean as new optional props to the `ClipAction` component. Also pass `onTrimToMediaEnd` and `onConvertToHold` callback props (wired in T7). Note: the registry is accessible via `current.resolvedConfig.registry[assetKey]?.duration` or `current.registry.assets[assetKey]?.duration` — check which path actually has the duration field populated by T4's changes.

Render overhang zone in `ClipAction.tsx`. Add `mediaEndFraction?: number | null` prop to ClipActionProps. When `mediaEndFraction` is a number between 0 and 1, render an overlay div inside the clip covering the rightmost `(1 - mediaEndFraction) * 100%` with a diagonal-stripe pattern via CSS `repeating-linear-gradient`. Use amber tones consistent with the existing `ring-amber-400/80` resize clamp indicator at `TrackListRenderer.tsx:124`: background `bg-amber-500/15`, stripe lines `bg-amber-500/25`. Add a thin 1-2px vertical divider at the `mediaEndFraction` position with `border-amber-500/50`. Add title attribute on hover: 'Dead time — media ends here. Right-click to trim or hold.' The overlay is a single positioned div inside the existing clip element — no new component files.

Add context menu actions for overhang clips in `ClipAction.tsx`. (1) Expand the context menu gate at line 364: `const canOpenContextMenu = !isInPinnedShotGroup || hasPinnedShotGroupAssetActions || hasOverhang;` where `hasOverhang = typeof mediaEndFraction === 'number' && mediaEndFraction < 1`. (2) Add two menu items OUTSIDE the `!props.isInPinnedShotGroup` guard (near the asset-state actions at lines 165-186 which are already visible for pinned clips): 'Trim to media end' (Scissors icon from lucide) calling `onTrimToMediaEnd` prop, and 'Hold last frame' (Pause icon or similar) calling `onConvertToHold` prop. Both only render when `hasOverhang`. Add a separator before them. (3) Wire callbacks from `TimelineEditor.tsx:711` getActionRender: trim calls `clampClipToMediaDuration` + updates `action.end` via `applyEdit`; hold calls `convertOverhangToHold` then applies the video patch + inserts the new hold clip action after the trimmed video, both in a single `applyEdit`.

Run all tests and verify. (1) Run `overhang.test.ts` — all unit tests must pass. (2) Run any existing tests related to the changed files: search for test files near `shot-group-commands`, `useExternalDrop`, `TimelineEditor`, `ClipAction`, `finalVideoAssets`, `config-utils`. Run them and fix any failures introduced by these changes. (3) Run the TypeScript compiler (`npx tsc --noEmit`) to catch type errors across the project. (4) Write a short throwaway test script that creates clip configs with overhang scenarios and verifies `detectClipOverhang`, `clampClipToMediaDuration`, and `convertOverhangToHold` produce correct results, run it, then delete it. (5) Verify no out-of-scope files were modified.

watch_items: Speed math: all overhang calculations must divide by `speed ?? 1` to convert source time to timeline time. Plan flag FLAG-001 was addressed but executor must verify every formula.

Pinned shot group menu gate: the `canOpenContextMenu` expansion (FLAG-002) must allow overhang actions while keeping OTHER menu items behind the `!isInPinnedShotGroup` guard. Only trim/hold escape the gate.
Asset registry duration: T4 must wire duration into `buildFinalVideoAssetEntry` so the registry has it when T5 looks it up. If the registry path is wrong, the overhang indicator will never fire.
No auto-clamp watcher: per FLAG-003, we are NOT building background retry infrastructure. Single retry at drop time + visual indicator is the design. Don't add reactive duration watchers.

Hold clip type reuse: the `convertOverhangToHold` helper produces a hold clip descriptor using the existing `hold` property and `clipType: 'hold'`. Verify the renderer already handles this clip type correctly for video assets.

getClipDurationSeconds naming: the plan references this name but it's actually a private function in `timeline-data.ts:107`, not exported from `config-utils.ts`. Use `getClipSourceDuration` (exported, line 17) and divide by speed manually.
Existing keyboard-delete files: there are uncommitted files `keyboard-delete.ts` and `keyboard-delete.test.ts` in the working tree — do not modify or delete these.
DEBT: final-video-duration-handling — this plan addresses the same domain. Ensure T3/T4 changes don't conflict with the existing duration resolution in `finalVideoAssets.ts:119-133`.

Context menu callback wiring: T7 wires `onTrimToMediaEnd` and `onConvertToHold` from TimelineEditor's getActionRender. The hold action must insert a new clip action — verify the `applyEdit` API supports inserting new actions, not just patching existing ones.

sense_checks.question: Does `detectClipOverhang` correctly return `hasOverhang: false` for hold clips, clips with missing assetDuration, AND clips where `to <= assetDuration`? Are all three no-overhang paths tested?
Does `convertOverhangToHold` produce a holdClip with `hold` value in timeline seconds (source overhang / speed), not source seconds? The hold property represents display duration.

Does the retry in useExternalDrop correctly await the 500ms delay without blocking the UI? Is it wrapped in the existing async flow, and does it handle the case where the component unmounts during the delay?

After T4, does `buildFinalVideoAssetEntry` include `duration` in the returned registry entry when the duration parameter is provided? Verify the AssetRegistryEntry type supports an optional `duration` field.

Does the registry lookup path in getActionRender actually find the duration value stored by T4? Trace: T4 stores duration in the asset entry → T5 reads it from `current.resolvedConfig.registry[key]?.duration` or equivalent. Are these the same data structure?

Is the overhang overlay positioned correctly with `position: absolute` inside a `position: relative` parent? Does the ClipAction component's root element already have relative positioning, or does it need to be added?

Are the trim/hold menu items placed OUTSIDE the `!isInPinnedShotGroup` conditional block but INSIDE the context menu component? Verify they render for pinned-group clips with overhang while other actions remain hidden.
Do all pre-existing tests pass without modification? If any tests were updated, were the changes limited to reflecting new clamping/retry behavior and not masking regressions?

meta_commentary:
Execution guidance:

1. **Start with T1+T2 (pure utilities)** — these have zero integration risk and establish the foundation. Run their tests immediately to validate the math before touching any integration code.

2. **T3 and T4 are independent of each other** and can be done in parallel after T1. T3 touches the drop path, T4 touches switch-to-final-video and registry wiring. Both are small, surgical changes.

3. **T5 is the critical bridge** — it connects the overhang utility to the renderer. The main risk is getting the registry lookup path right. The plan suggests two possible paths (`resolvedConfig.registry` vs `registry.assets`). Read the actual data structure at runtime or trace from T4's write location to find the correct read path.

4. **T6 and T7 are the UI-facing work.** T6 is purely visual (overlay rendering). T7 is the most complex task — it touches both ClipAction (menu items) and TimelineEditor (callback wiring). The hold action is the hardest part because it needs to insert a NEW clip action into the timeline, not just patch an existing one. Study how existing clip insertion works (e.g., `useAssetManagement.ts:201-252`) to find the right `applyEdit` pattern.

5. **Key naming pitfall**: The plan says `getClipDurationSeconds` but this doesn't exist as a public API. Use `getClipSourceDuration(clipMeta)` from `config-utils.ts:17` and divide by `speed ?? 1` yourself. The private `getClipDurationSeconds` in `timeline-data.ts:107` does the same thing but isn't exported.

6. **Don't touch** the uncommitted `keyboard-delete.ts` and `keyboard-delete.test.ts` files or the `.megaplan/` directory.

plan_steps_covered.plan_step_summary: Step 1: Create overhang detection utility (detectClipOverhang, getMediaEndFraction) with tests
Step 2: Add clampClipToMediaDuration and convertOverhangToHold helpers with tests
Step 3: Improve duration resolution on shot drop with single retry
Step 4: Clamp on switch-to-final-video and wire duration into asset registry
Step 5: Pass overhang data (mediaEndFraction, hasOverhang) to clip renderer
Step 6: Render overhang zone visual overlay in ClipAction
Step 7: Add context menu actions (Trim to media end, Hold last frame) with pinned-group gate expansion
Step 8: Update tests and validate all changes

validation.completeness_notes: All 8 plan steps are covered. Steps 1-2 map to T1-T2 (pure utilities). Steps 3-4 map to T3-T4 (prevention). Steps 5-6 map to T5-T6 (visual). Step 7 maps to T7 (user actions). Step 8 maps to T8 (validation). The plan's execution order is preserved in task dependencies.

baseline_test_note: Baseline test command targets the video-editor tool directory. Run before starting work to capture any pre-existing failures. The overhang.test.ts file does not exist yet so it won't be included in baseline.
